Notice Of Motion And Motion For A Protective Order For The Deposition Of Dr Anthony Reading To Occur Remotely Declaration Of Alex Dibona Esq Exhibits
Off Calendar Apr-29-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF DANIELLE KESICH's Motion For A Protective Order For The Deposition Of Dr Anthony Reading To Occur Remotely.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Danielle Di Bari's motion for a protective order allowing her retained expert Dr. Anthony Reading to be deposed remotely is denied. CCP 2034.420 states, "The deposition of any expert described in subdivision (b) of Section 2034.210 shall be taken at a place that is within 75 miles of the courthouse where the action is pending. On motion for a protective order by the party designating an expert witness, and on a showing of exceptional hardship, the court may order that the deposition be taken at a more distant place from the courthouse." Di Bari does not make a specific showing of "exceptional hardship." Her showing is based on (1) defendants' initial acquiescence to a remote deposition; and (2) the assertion that Reading continues to be affected by the Los Angeles wildfires and the loss of his home. The court is certainly sympathetic, but given the passage of time and the lack of specific showing how Reading would be affected by having to travel, finds that the latter reason does not suffice as a showing of exceptional hardship. The former assertion is not relevant, as the deposition where defendants agreed to depose Reading remotely did not occur. Defendants are not required to make a showing of prejudice from a remote deposition; rather, the burden is on Di Bari to show reason to excuse her expert's compliance with 2034.420.

Defendants seek sanctions. CCP 2025.420(h) provides that "The court shall impose a monetary sanction under Chapter 7 (commencing with Section 2023.010) against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully makes or opposes a motion for a protective order, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." The court does not find Di Bari acted with substantial justification; her position on this motion was not supported by authorities. The court awards $1000 in sanctions payable by Di Bari to defendants payable within 30 days of this order. Defendants seek a much higher amount but the court finds the amount of time Defendants spent on this straightforward motion to be unreasonable and does not award the full amount.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Hearing re: PLAINTIFF DANIELLE KESICH's Motion For Protective Order To Prevent Defendants From Taking The Deposition Of Ekaterina Alegina.

Matter not argued/contested. The court adopts its tentative ruling.

The motion is granted in part. The deposition of Ekaterina Alegina shall go forward at a mutually agreeable time and place. Defendants may ask questions regarding, among other topics, Ms. Alegina's schedule, her actual hours worked, and anything else she is a percipient witness to. However, during the deposition, Ms. Alegina shall not testify regarding (1) the details of her compensation or (2) anything about Plaintiff's minor child, and Plaintiff's counsel shall be entitled to so instruct Ms. Alegina during the deposition. This order is without prejudice to Plaintiff seeking a further protective order on privacy grounds to the extent that, during the deposition, Defendants seek other private information that is not relevant to this action. Defendants' request for sanctions is denied.

Counsel for the prevailing party is to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the court's ruling and email it to Judge Pro Tem Matthew Kahn.
